# KG ingestion gate hard-FAILs legacy approved docs on major upgrade; bare entity-id mention mis-classified as definition

## Summary

Upgrading scaffold 0.4.1 -> 0.9.1 ran kg-init (kg-first strategy) for the first time and activated the new 'KG ingestion completeness' doctor check as a hard FAIL against pre-existing approved documents that never followed the KG define-once/xref convention. The importer's entity_extract treats ANY bare T-NNN/F-NNN/AC-NNN occurrence (outside an xref form or code block) as a *definition*, so legitimate reference patterns — a test-report coverage matrix listing task/AC ids in table cells, and a dev-plan task heading naming the feature it implements (### T-087: F-005 ...) — collide with their canonical prd/dev-plan definitions and abort the whole import (14 entity_id collisions). A major-version upgrade should not silently turn a brand-new mandatory gate red on legacy approved deliverables with no migration path.

## Environment

- **CataForge package**: `0.9.1`
- **Scaffold version**: `0.9.1`
- **Python**: `3.14.4`
- **Platform**: `Windows-11-10.0.26200-SP0`
- **Runtime platform**: `claude-code`

## Proposal

Two concrete asks: (1) Migration ergonomics — when a major upgrade first activates KG ingestion against existing docs, provide a migration command (e.g. cataforge kg migrate-refs) that auto-converts cross-document bare entity-id mentions to xref form or inline-code, instead of a hard doctor FAIL the user must hand-fix across approved documents. Alternatively gate the check behind an explicit opt-in for projects whose docs predate KG. (2) Importer heuristic — 'any bare entity-id occurrence = definition' over-triggers on legitimate reference surfaces. Reference-only doc_types (test-report coverage matrices, sprint reports) should be treated as pure-reference (extract relations, not definitions), and a heading that merely names another entity in its title text (### T-087: F-005 ...) should not be read as redefining F-005. Workaround applied downstream: wrap the bare ids in inline code (importer excludes code spans), which clears the gate but is a hack, not define-once unification. (3) Doctor's dangling-WARN over-reports — and the cause is NOT under-registered doc_types (my first read was wrong). The dangling scan (`_scan_fs_entity_ids`) builds its "referenced" set with the FULL entity-prefix regex, but several prefixes map to relation-only / metadata classes that are NEVER written as entity nodes: TestCase (TC), CoverageRule (CR), SprintReviewIssue (SR) participate solely in relation extraction (e.g. `TestCase cf:verifies T/AC/F`) and produce zero nodes in the store. So any active doc that merely lists TC-/CR-/SR- ids — a test-report coverage matrix is 71 `TC-` ids by itself — is STRUCTURALLY guaranteed to be "dangling", regardless of how the doc is written. 76 of our 79 were exactly this (TCx71 + CRx4 + SRx1); the remaining 3 were genuine stale references to arch-deleted API numbers (API-010/026/029), fixed downstream by inline-coding. Fix: restrict the dangling check's "referenced" set to prefixes whose class is actually entity-ised (exclude relation-only classes), so doctor stops flagging ids the importer is designed never to ingest. The dangling candidate set is currently a superset of the KG's entity-ised class set — that asymmetry is the bug.
